title: VII: The Fracas in the Tavern
html:
VII

The Fracas in the Tavern

I

Yvonne had settled herself in a corner of the taproom on a bench and had tried to lose consciousness of her surroundings.

It was not easy! Glances charged with rancour were levelled at her dainty appearanceпїњвАФdainty and refined despite the look of starvation and of weariness on her face and the miserable state of her clothingпїњвАФand not a few muttered insults waited on those glances.

As soon as she was seated Yvonne noticed that the old man and the coarse, fat woman behind the bar started an animated conversation together, of which she was very obviously the object, for the two headsпїњвАФthe lean and the roundпїњвАФwere jerked more than once in her direction. Presently the manпїњвАФit was George Lemoine, the proprietor of the Rat MortпїњвАФcame up to where she was sitting: his lank figure was bent so that his lean back formed the best part of an arc, and an expression of mock deference further distorted his ugly face.

He came up quite close to Yvonne and she found it passing difficult not to draw away from him, for the leer on his face was appalling: his eyes, which were set very near to his hooked nose, had a horrible squint, his lips were thick and moist, and his breath reeked of alcohol.

вАЬWhat will the noble lady deign to drink?вАЭ he now asked in an oily, suave voice.

And Yvonne, remembering the guideвАЩs admonitions, contrived to smile unconcernedly into the hideous face.

вАЬI would very much like some wine,вАЭ she said cheerfully, вАЬbut I am afraid that I have no money wherewith to pay you for it.вАЭ

The creature with a gesture of abject humility rubbed his greasy hands together.

вАЬAnd may I respectfully ask,вАЭ he queried blandly, вАЬwhat are the intentions of the noble lady in coming to this humble abode, if she hath no desire to partake of refreshments?вАЭ

вАЬI am expecting friends,вАЭ replied Yvonne bravely; вАЬthey will be here very soon, and will gladly repay you lavishly for all the kindness which you may be inclined to show to me the while.вАЭ

She was very brave indeed and looked this awful misshapen specimen of a man quite boldly in the face: she even contrived to smile, though she was well aware that a number of men and womenпїњвАФperhaps a dozen altogetherпїњвАФhad congregated in front of her in a compact group around the landlord, that they were nudging one another and pointing derisivelyпїњвАФmalevolentlyпїњвАФat her. It was impossible, despite all attempts at valour, to mistake the hostile attitude of these people. Some of the most obscene words, coined during these last horrible days of the Revolution, were freely hurled at her, and one woman suddenly cried out in a shrill treble:

вАЬThrow her out, citizen Lemoine! We donвАЩt want spies in here!вАЭ

вАЬIndeed, indeed,вАЭ said Yvonne as quietly as she could, вАЬI am no spy. I am poor and wreched like yourselves! and desperately lonely, save for the kind friends who will meet me here anon.вАЭ

вАЬAristos like yourself!вАЭ growled one of the men. вАЬThis is no place for you or for them.вАЭ

вАЬNo! No! This is no place for aristos,вАЭ cried one of the women in a voice which many excesses and many vices had rendered hoarse and rough. вАЬSpy or not, we donвАЩt want you in here. Do we?вАЭ she added as with arms akimbo she turned to face those of her own sex, who behind the men had come up in order to see what was going on.

вАЬThrow her out, Lemoine,вАЭ reiterated a man who appeared to be an oracle amongst the others.

вАЬPlease! please let me stop here!вАЭ pleaded Yvonne; вАЬif you turn me out I shall not know what to do: I shall not know where to meet my friendsпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

вАЬPretty story about those friends,вАЭ broke in Lemoine roughly. вАЬHow do I know if youвАЩre lying or not?вАЭ

From the opposite angle of the room, the woman behind the bar had been watching the little scene with eyes that glistened with cupidity. Now she emerged from behind her stronghold of bottles and mugs and slowly waddled across the room. She pushed her way unceremoniously past her customers, elbowing men, women and children vigorously aside with a deft play of her large, muscular arms. Having reached the forefront of the little group she came to a standstill immediately in front of Yvonne, and crossing her mighty arms over her ponderous chest she eyed the вАЬaristoвАЭ with unconcealed malignity.

вАЬWe do know that the slut is lyingпїњвАФthat is where you make the mistake, Lemoine. A slut, thatвАЩs what she isпїњвАФand the friend whom sheвАЩs going to meetпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАК? Well!вАЭ she added, turning with an ugly leer toward the other women, вАЬwe all know what sort of friend that one is likely to be, eh, mesdames? Bringing evil fame on this house, thatвАЩs what the wench is afterпїњвАКпїњвА¶ so as to bring the police about our earsпїњвАКпїњвА¶ I wouldnвАЩt trust her, not another minute. Out with you and at onceпїњвАФdo you hear?пїњвАКпїњвА¶ this instantпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Lemoine has parleyed quite long enough with you already!вАЭ

Despite all her resolutions Yvonne was terribly frightened. While the hideous old hag talked and screamed and waved her coarse, red arms about, the unfortunate young girl with a great effort of will, kept repeating to herself: вАЬI am not frightenedпїњвАФI must not be frightened. He assured me that these people would do me no harm.пїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ But now when the woman had ceased speaking there was a general murmur of:

вАЬThrow her out! Spy or aristo we donвАЩt want her here!вАЭ whilst some of the men added significantly: вАЬI am sure that she is one of CarrierвАЩs spies and in league with his Marats! We shall have those devils in here in a moment if we donвАЩt look out! Throw her out before she can signal to the Marats!вАЭ

Ugly faces charged with hatred and virulence were thrust threateningly forwardпїњвАФone or two of the women were obviously looking forward to joining in the scramble, when this вАЬstuck-up wenchвАЭ would presently be hurled out into the street.

вАЬNow then, my girl, out you get,вАЭ concluded the woman Lemoine, as with an expressive gesture she proceeded to roll her sleeves higher up her arm. She was about to lay her dirty hands on Yvonne, and the poor girl was nearly sick with horror, when one of the menпїњвАФa huge, coarse giant, whose muscular torso, covered with grease and grime showed almost naked through a ragged shirt which hung from his shoulders in stripsпїњвАФseized the woman Lemoine by the arm and dragged her back a step or two away from Yvonne.

вАЬDonвАЩt be a fool, petite m√®re,вАЭ he said, accompanying this admonition with a blasphemous oath. вАЬSlut or no, the wench may as well pay you something for the privilege of staying here. Look at that cloak sheвАЩs wearingпїњвАФthe shoe-leather on her feet. ArenвАЩt they worth a bottle of your sour wine?вАЭ

вАЬWhatвАЩs that to you, Paul Friche?вАЭ retorted the woman roughly, as with a vigorous gesture she freed her arm from the manвАЩs grasp. вАЬIs this my house or yours?вАЭ

вАЬYours, of course,вАЭ replied the man with a coarse laugh and a still coarser jest, вАЬbut this wonвАЩt be the first time that I have saved you from impulsive folly. Yesterday you were for harbouring a couple of rogues who were Marats in disguise: if I hadnвАЩt given you warning, you would now have swallowed more water from the Loire than you would care to hold. But for me two days ago you would have received the goods pinched by Fert√© out of BalazeвАЩs shop, and been thrown to the fishes in consequence for the entertainment of the proconsul and his friends. You must admit that IвАЩve been a good friend to you before now.вАЭ

вАЬAnd if you have, Paul Friche,вАЭ retorted the hag obstinately, вАЬI paid you well for your friendship, both yesterday and the day before, didnвАЩt I?вАЭ

вАЬYou did,вАЭ assented Friche imperturbably. вАЬThatвАЩs why I want to serve you again tonight.вАЭ

вАЬDonвАЩt listen to him, petite m√®re,вАЭ interposed one of two out of the crowd. вАЬHe is a white-livered skunk to talk to you like that.вАЭ

вАЬVery well! Very well!вАЭ quoth Paul Friche, and he spat vigorously on the ground in token that henceforth he divested himself from any responsibility in this matter, вАЬdonвАЩt listen to me. Lose a benefit of twenty, perhaps forty francs for the sake of a bit of fun. Very well! Very well!вАЭ he continued as he turned and slouched out of the group to the further end of the room, where he sat down on a barrel. He drew the stump of a clay pipe out of the pocket of his breeches, stuffed it into his mouth, stretched his long legs out before him and sucked away at his pipe with complacent detachment. вАЬI didnвАЩt know,вАЭ he added with biting sarcasm by way of a parting shot, вАЬthat you and Lemoine had come into a fortune recently and that forty or fifty francs are nothing to you now.вАЭ

вАЬForty or fifty? Come! come!вАЭ protested Lemoine feebly.

II

YvonneвАЩs fate was hanging in the balance. The attitude of the small crowd was no less threatening than before, but immediate action was withheld while the Lemoines obviously debated in their minds what was best to be done. The instinct to вАЬhave atвАЭ an aristo with all the accumulated hatred of many generations was warring with the innate rapacity of the Breton peasant.

вАЬForty or fifty?вАЭ reiterated Paul Friche emphatically. вАЬCanвАЩt you see that the wench is an aristo escaped out of Le Bouffay or the entrep√іt?вАЭ he added contemptuously.

вАЬI know that she is an aristo,вАЭ said the woman, вАЬthatвАЩs why I want to throw her out.вАЭ

вАЬAnd get nothing for your pains,вАЭ retorted Friche roughly. вАЬIf you wait for her friends we may all of us get as much as twenty francs each to hold our tongues.вАЭ

вАЬTwenty francs each.пїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ The murmur was repeated with many a sigh of savage gluttony, by everyone in the roomпїњвАФand repeated again and againпїњвАФespecially by the women.

вАЬYou are a fool, Paul FricheпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ commented Lemoine.

вАЬA fool am I?вАЭ retorted the giant. вАЬThen let me tell you, that вАЩtis you who are a fool and worse. I happen to know,вАЭ he added, as he once more rose and rejoined the group in the centre of the room, вАЬI happen to know that you and everyone here is heading straight for a trap arranged by the Committee of Public Safety, whose chief emissary came into Nantes awhile ago and is named Chauvelin. It is a trap which will land you all in the criminal dock first and on the way to Cayenne or the guillotine afterwards. This place is surrounded with Marats, and orders have been issued to them to make a descent on this place, as soon as papa LemoineвАЩs customers are assembled. There are two members of the accursed company amongst us at the present moment.пїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

He was standing right in the middle of the room, immediately beneath the hanging lamp. At his wordsпїњвАФspoken with such firm confidence, as one who knows and is therefore empowered to speakпїњвАФa sudden change came over the spirit of the whole assembly. Everything was forgotten in the face of this new dangerпїњвАФtwo Marats, the sleuthhounds of the proconsulпїњвАФhere present, as spies and as informants! Every face became more haggardпїњвАФevery cheek more livid. There was a quick and furtive scurrying toward the front door.

вАЬTwo Marats here?вАЭ shouted one man, who was bolder than the rest. вАЬWhere are they?вАЭ

Paul Friche, who towered above his friends, stood at this moment quite close to a small man, dressed like the others in ragged breeches and shirt, and wearing the broad-brimmed hat usually affected by the Breton peasantry.

вАЬTwo Marats? Two spies?вАЭ screeched a woman. вАЬWhere are they?вАЭ

вАЬHere is one,вАЭ replied Paul Friche with a loud laugh: and with his large grimy hand he lifted the hat from his neighbourвАЩs head and threw it on the ground; вАЬand there,вАЭ he added as with long, bony finger he pointed to the front door, where another manпїњвАФa square-built youngster with tow-coloured hair somewhat resembling a shaggy dogпїњвАФwas endeavouring to effect a surreptitious exit, вАЬthere is the other; and he is on the point of slipping quietly away in order to report to his captain what he has seen and heard at the Rat Mort. One moment, citizen,вАЭ he added, and with a couple of giant strides he too had reached the door; his large rough hand had come down heavily on the shoulder of the youth with the tow-coloured hair, and had forced him to veer round and to face the angry, gesticulating crowd.

вАЬTwo Marats! Two spies!вАЭ shouted the men. вАЬNow weвАЩll soon settle their little business for them!вАЭ

вАЬMarat yourself,вАЭ cried the small man who had first been denounced by Friche. вАЬI am no Marat, as a good many of you here know. Maman Lemoine,вАЭ he added pleading, вАЬyou know me. Am I a Marat?вАЭ

But the LemoinesпїњвАФman and wifeпїњвАФat the first suggestion of police had turned a deaf ear to all their customers. Their own safety being in jeopardy they cared little what happened to anybody else. They had retired behind their counter and were in close consultation together, no doubt as to the best means of escape if indeed the man Paul Friche spoke the truth.

вАЬI know nothing about him,вАЭ the woman was saying, вАЬbut he certainly was right last night about those two men who came ferreting in hereпїњвАФand last week tooпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

вАЬAm I a Marat, maman Lemoine?вАЭ shouted the small man as he hammered his fists upon the counter. вАЬFor ten years and more I have been a customer in this place andпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

вАЬAm I a Marat?вАЭ shouted the youth with the tow-coloured hair addressing the assembly indiscriminately. вАЬSome of you here know me well enough. Jean Paul, you knowпїњвАФLedouble, you tooпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

вАЬOf course! Of course I know you well enough, Jacques Leroux,вАЭ came with a loud laugh from one of the crowd. вАЬWho said you were a Marat?вАЭ

вАЬAm I a Marat, maman Lemoine?вАЭ reiterated the small man at the counter.

вАЬOh! leave me alone with your quarrels,вАЭ shouted the woman Lemoine in reply. вАЬSettle them among yourselves.вАЭ

вАЬThen if Jacques Leroux is not a Marat,вАЭ now came in a bibulous voice from a distant comer of the room, вАЬand this compeer here is known to maman Lemoine, where are the real Marats who according to this fellow Friche, whom we none of us know, are spying upon us?вАЭ

вАЬYes! where are they?вАЭ suggested another. вАЬShow вАЩem to us, Paul Friche, or whatever your accursed name happens to be.вАЭ

вАЬTell us where you come from yourself,вАЭ screamed the woman with the shrill treble, вАЬit seems to me quite possible that youвАЩre a Marat yourself.вАЭ

This suggestion was at once taken up.

вАЬMarat yourself!вАЭ shouted the crowd, and the two men who a moment ago had been accused of being spies in disguise shouted louder than the rest: вАЬMarat yourself!вАЭ

III

After that, pandemonium reigned.

The words вАЬpoliceвАЭ and вАЬMaratsвАЭ had aroused the terror of all these nighthawks, who were wont to think themselves immune inside their lair: and terror is at all times an evil counsellor. In the space of a few seconds confusion held undisputed sway. Everyone screamed, waved arms, stamped feet, struck out with heavy bare fists at his nearest neighbour. EveryoneвАЩs hand was against everyone else.

вАЬSpy! Marat! Informer!вАЭ were the three words that detached themselves most clearly from out the babel of vituperations freely hurled from end to end of the room.

The children screamed, the womenвАЩs shrill or hoarse treble mingled with the cries and imprecations of the men.

Paul Friche had noted that the turn of the tide was against him, long before the first naked fist had been brandished in his face. Agile as a monkey he had pushed his way through to the bar, and placing his two hands upon it, with a swift leap he had taken up a sitting position in the very middle of the table amongst the jugs and bottles, which he promptly seized and used as missiles and weapons, whilst with his dangling feet encased in heavy sabots he kicked out vigorously and unceasingly against the shins of his foremost assailants.

He had the advantage of position and used it cleverly. In his right hand he held a pewter mug by the handle and used it as a swivel against his aggressors with great effect.

вАЬThe Loire for youпїњвАФyou blackmailer! liar! traitor!вАЭ shouted some of the women who, bolder than the men, thrust shaking fists at Paul Friche as closely as that pewter mug would allow.

вАЬBreak his jaw before he can yell for the police,вАЭ admonished one of the men from the rear, вАЬbefore he can save his own skin.вАЭ

But those who shouted loudest had only their fists by way of weapon and Paul Friche had mugs and bottles, and those sabots of his kicked out with uncomfortable agility.

вАЬBreak my jaw, will you,вАЭ he shouted every time that a blow from the mug went home, вАЬa spy am I? Very well then, hereвАЩs for you, Jacques Leroux; go and nurse your cracked skull at home. You want a row,вАЭ he added hitting at a youth who brandished a heavy fist in his face, вАЬwell! you shall have it and as much of it as you like! as much of it as will bring the patrols of police comfortably about your ears.вАЭ

Bang! went the pewter mug crashing against a manвАЩs hard skull! Bang went Paul FricheвАЩs naked fist against the chest of another. He was a hard hitter and swift.

The Lemoines from behind their bar shouted louder than the rest, doing as much as their lungs would allow them in the way of admonishing, entreating, protestingпїњвАФcursing everyone for a set of fools who were playing straight into the hands of the police.

вАЬNow then! Now then, children, stop that bellowing, will you? There are no spies here. Paul Friche was only having his little joke! We all know one another, what?вАЭ

вАЬCamels!вАЭ added Lemoine more forcibly. вАЬTheyвАЩll bring the patrols about our ears for sure.вАЭ

Paul Friche was not by any means the only man who was being vigorously attacked. After the first two or three minutes of this kingdom of pandemonium, it was difficult to say who was quarrelling with whom. Old grudges were revived, old feuds taken up there, where they had previously been interrupted. Accusations of spying were followed by abuse for some past wrong of blacklegging or cheating a confr√®re. The temperature of the room became suffocating. All these violent passions seething within these four walls seemed to become tangible and to mingle with the atmosphere already surcharged with the fumes of alcohol, of tobacco and of perspiring humanity. There was many a black-eye already, many a contusion: more than one knifeпїњвАФsurreptitiously drawnпїњвАФwas already stained with red.

IV

There was also a stampede for the door. One man gave the signal. Seeing that his mates were wasting precious time by venting their wrath against Paul Friche and then quarrelling among themselves, he hoped to effect an escape ere the police came to stop the noise. No one believed in the place being surrounded. Why should it be? The Marats were far too busy hunting up rebels and aristos to trouble much about the Rat Mort and its customers, but it was quite possible that a brawl would bring a patrol along, and then вАЩware the police correctionnelle and the possibility of deportation or worse. Retreat was undoubtedly safer while there was time. One man first: then one or two more on his heels, and those among the women who had children in their arms or clinging to their skirts: they turned stealthily to the doorпїњвАФalmost ashamed of their cowardice, ashamed lest they were seen abandoning the field of combat.

It was while confusion reigned unchecked that YvonneпїњвАФwho was cowering, frankly terrified at last, in the corner of the room, became aware that the door close beside herпїњвАФthe door situated immediately opposite the front entranceпїњвАФwas surreptitiously opened. She turned quickly to lookпїњвАФfor she was like a terror-stricken little animal nowпїњвАФone that scents and feels and fears danger from every quarter round. The door was being pushed open very slowly by what was still to Yvonne an unseen hand. Somehow that opening door fascinated her: for the moment she forgot the noise and the confusion around her.

Then suddenly with a great effort of will she checked the scream which had forced itself up to her throat.

вАЬFather!вАЭ was all that she contrived to say in a hoarse and passionate murmur.

Fortunately as he peered cautiously round the room, M. le duc caught sight of his daughter. She was staring at himпїњвАФwide-eyed, her lips bloodless, her cheeks the colour of ashes. He looked but the ghost now of that proud aristocrat who little more than a week ago was the centre of a group of courtiers round the person of the heir to the English throne. Starved, emaciated, livid, he was the shadow of his former self, and there was a haunted look in his purple-rimmed eyes which spoke with pathetic eloquence of sleepless nights and of a soul tortured with remorse.

Just for the moment no one took any notice of himпїњвАФeveryone was shrieking, everyone was quarrelling, and M. le duc, placing a finger to his lips, stole cautiously round to his daughter. The next instant they were clinging to one another, these two, who had endured so much togetherпїњвАФhe the father who had wrought such an unspeakable wrong, and she the child who was so lonely, so forlorn and almost happy in finding someone who belonged to her, someone to whom she could cling.

вАЬFather, dear! what shall we do?вАЭ Yvonne murmured, for she felt the last shred of her fictitious courage oozing out of her, in face of this awful lawlessness which literally paralysed her thinking faculties.

вАЬSh! dear!вАЭ whispered M. le duc in reply. вАЬWe must get out of this loathsome place while this hideous row is going on. I heard it all from the filthy garret up above, where those devils have kept me these three days. The door was not lockedпїњвАКпїњвА¶ I crept downstairsпїњвАКпїњвА¶ No one is paying heed to usпїњвАКпїњвА¶ We can creep out. Come.вАЭ

But at the suggestion, YvonneвАЩs spirits, which had been stunned by the events of the past few moments, revived with truly mercurial rapidity.

вАЬNo! no! dear,вАЭ she urged. вАЬWe must stay hereпїњвАКпїњвА¶ You donвАЩt knowпїњвАКпїњвА¶ I have had a messageпїњвАФfrom my own dear milorпїњвАФmy husbandпїњвАКпїњвА¶ he sent a friend to take me out of the hideous prison where that awful Pierre Adet was keeping meпїњвАФa friend who assured me that my dear milor was watching over meпїњвАКпїњвА¶ he brought me to this placeпїњвАФand begged me not to be frightenedпїњвАКпїњвА¶ but to wait patientlyпїњвАКпїњвА¶ and I must wait, dearпїњвАКпїњвА¶ I must wait!вАЭ

She spoke rapidly in whispers and in short jerky sentences. M. le duc listened to her wide-eyed, a deep line of puzzlement between his brows. Sorrow, remorse, starvation, misery had in a measure numbed his mind. The thought of help, of hope, of friends could not penetrate into his brain.

вАЬA message,вАЭ he murmured inanely, вАЬa message. No! no! my girl, you must trust no one.пїњвАКпїњвА¶ Pierre Adet.пїњвАКпїњвА¶ Pierre Adet is full of evil tricksпїњвАФhe will trap youпїњвАКпїњвА¶ he means to destroy us bothпїњвАКпїњвА¶ he has brought you here so that you should be murdered by these ferocious devils.вАЭ

вАЬImpossible, father dear,вАЭ she said, still striving to speak bravely. вАЬWe have both of us been all this while in the power of Pierre Adet; he could have had no object in bringing me here tonight.вАЭ

But the father who had been an insentient tool in the schemes of that miserable intriguer, who had been the means of bringing his only child to this terrible and deadly passпїњвАФthe man who had listened to the lying counsels and proposals of his own most bitter enemy, could only groan now in terror and in doubt.

вАЬWho can probe the depths of that abominable villainвАЩs plans?вАЭ he murmured vaguely.

In the meanwhile the little group who had thought prudence the better part of valour had reached the door. The foremost man amongst them opened it and peered cautiously out into the darkness. He turned back to those behind him, put a finger to his lip and beckoned to them to follow him in silence.

вАЬYvonne, let us go!вАЭ whispered the duc, who had seized his daughter by the hand.

вАЬBut fatherпїњвАКпїњвА¶вАЭ

вАЬLet us go!вАЭ he reiterated pitiably. вАЬI shall die if we stay here!вАЭ

вАЬIt wonвАЩt be for long, father dear,вАЭ she entreated; вАЬif milor should come with his friend, and find us gone, we should be endangering his life as well as our own.вАЭ

вАЬI donвАЩt believe it,вАЭ he rejoined with the obstinacy of weakness. вАЬI donвАЩt believe in your messageпїњвАКпїњвА¶ how could milor or anyone come to your rescue, my child?пїњвАКпїњвА¶ No one knows that you are here, in this hell in Nantes.вАЭ

Yvonne clung to him with the strength of despair. She too was as terrified as any human creature could be and live, but terror had not altogether swept away her belief in that mysterious message, in that tall guide who had led her hither, in that scarlet deviceпїњвАФthe five-petalled flower which stood for everything that was most gallant and most brave.

She desired with all her might to remain hereпїњвАФdespite everything, despite the awful brawl that was raging round her and which sickened her, despite the horror of the whole thingпїњвАФto remain here and to wait. She put her arms round her father: she dragged him back every time that he tried to move. But a sort of unnatural strength seemed to have conquered his former debility. His attempts to get away became more and more determined and more and more febrile.

вАЬCome, Yvonne! we must go!вАЭ he continued to murmur intermittently and with ever-growing obstinacy. вАЬNo one will notice usпїњвАКпїњвА¶ I heard the noise from my garret upstairsпїњвАКпїњвА¶ I crept downпїњвАКпїњвА¶ I knew no one would notice meпїњвАКпїњвА¶ ComeпїњвАФwe must goпїњвАКпїњвА¶ now is our time.вАЭ

вАЬFather, dear, whither could we go? Once in the streets of Nantes what would happen to us?вАЭ

вАЬWe can find our way to the Loire!вАЭ he retorted almost brutally. He shook himself free from her restraining arms and gripped her firmly by the hand. He tried to drag her toward the door, whilst she still struggled to keep him back. He had just caught sight of the group of men and women at the front door: their leader was standing upon the threshold and was still peering out into the darkness.

But the next moment they all came to a halt: what their leader had perceived through the darkness did not evidently quite satisfy him: he turned and held a whispered consultation with the others. M. le duc strove with all his might to join in with that group. He felt that in its wake would lie the road to freedom. He would have struck Yvonne for standing in the way of her own safety.

вАЬFather dear,вАЭ she contrived finally to say to him, вАЬif you go hence, you will go alone. Nothing will move me from here, because I know that milor will come.вАЭ

вАЬCurse you for your obstinacy,вАЭ retorted the duc, вАЬyou jeopardise my life and yours.вАЭ

Then suddenly from the angle of the room where wrangling and fighting were at their fiercest, there came a loud call:

вАЬLook out, p√®re Lemoine, your aristos are running away. You are losing your last chance of those fifty francs.вАЭ

It was Paul Friche who had shouted. His position on the table was giving him a commanding view over the heads of the threatening, shouting, perspiring crowd, and he had just caught sight of M. le duc dragging his daughter by force toward the door.

вАЬThe authors of all this pother,вАЭ he added with an oath, вАЬand they will get away whilst we have the police about our ears.вАЭ

вАЬName of a name of a dog,вАЭ swore Lemoine from behind his bar, вАЬthat shall not be. Come along, maman, let us bring those aristos along here. Quick now.вАЭ

It was all done in a second. Lemoine and his wife, with the weight and authority of the masters of the establishment, contrived to elbow their way through the crowd. The next moment Yvonne felt herself forcibly dragged away from her father.

вАЬThis way, my girl, and no screaming,вАЭ a bibulous voice said in her ear, вАЬno screaming, or IвАЩll smash some of those front teeth of yours. You said some rich friends were coming along for you presently. Well then! come and wait for them out of the crowd!вАЭ

Indeed Yvonne had no desire to struggle or to scream. Salvation she thought had come to her and to her father in this rough guise. In another moment mayhap he would have forced her to follow him, to leave milor in the lurch, to jeopardise forever every chance of safety.

вАЬIt is all for the best, father dear,вАЭ she managed to cry out over her shoulder, for she had just caught sight of him being seized round the shoulders by Lemoine and heard him protesting loudly:

вАЬIвАЩll not go! IвАЩll not go! Let me go!вАЭ he shouted hoarsely. вАЬMy daughter! Yvonne! Let me go! You devil!вАЭ

But Lemoine had twice the vigour of the duc de Kernogan, nor did he care one jot about the otherвАЩs protests. He hated all this row inside his house, but there had been rows in it before and he was beginning to hope that nothing serious would come of it. On the other hand, Paul Friche might be right about these aristos; there might be forty or fifty francs to be made out of them, and in any case they had one or two things upon their persons which might be worth a few francsпїњвАФand who knows? they might even have something in their pockets worth taking.

This hope and thought gave Lemoine additional strength, and seeing that the aristo struggled so desperately, he thought to silence him by bringing his heavy fist with a crash upon the old manвАЩs head.

вАЬYvonne! A moi!вАЭ shouted M. le duc ere he fell back senseless.

That awful cry, Yvonne heard it as she was being dragged through the noisome crowd. It mingled in her ear with the other awful soundsпїњвАФthe oaths and blasphemies which filled the air with their hideousness. It died away just as a formidable crash against the entrance door suddenly silenced every cry within.

вАЬAll hands up!вАЭ came with a peremptory word of command from the doorway.

вАЬMercy on us!вАЭ murmured the woman Lemoine, who still had Yvonne by the hand, вАЬwe are undone this time.вАЭ

There was a clatter and grounding of armsпїњвАФa scurrying of bare feet and sabots upon the floor, the mingled sounds of men trying to fly and being caught in the act and hurled back: screams of terror from the women, one or two pitiable calls, a few shrill cries from frightened children, a few dull thuds as of human bodies falling.пїњвАКпїњвА¶ It was all so confused, so unspeakably horrible. Yvonne was hardly conscious. Near her someone whispered hurriedly:

вАЬPut the aristos away somewhere, maman LemoineпїњвАКпїњвА¶ the whole thing may only be a scareпїњвАКпїњвА¶ the Marats may only be here about the aristosпїњвАКпїњвА¶ they will probably leave you alone if you give them upпїњвАКпїњвА¶ perhaps youвАЩll get a rewardпїњвАКпїњвА¶ Put them away till some of this row subsidesпїњвАКпїњвА¶ IвАЩll talk to commandant Fleury if I can.вАЭ

Yvonne felt her knees giving way under her. There was nothing more to hope for nowпїњвАФnothing. She felt herself lifted from the groundпїњвАФshe was too sick and faint to realise what was happening: through the din which filled her ears she vainly tried to distinguish her fatherвАЩs voice again.

V

A moment or two later she found herself squatting somewhere on the ground. How she got here she did not knowпїњвАФwhere she was she knew still less. She was in total darkness. A fusty, close smell of food and wine gave her a wretched feeling of nauseaпїњвАФher head ached intolerably, her eyes were hot, her throat dry: there was a constant buzzing in her ears.

The terrible sounds of fighting and screaming and cursing, the crash of broken glass and overturned benches came to her as through a partitionпїњвАФclose by but muffled.

In the immediate nearness all was silence and darkness.
